text:
In 1985, a Costco warehouse in Seattle, Washington, listed a hot dog and a twenty-ounce soda for one dollar and fifty cents. That was forty-one years ago. The price today is still one dollar and fifty cents.

In the same period, the US Consumer Price Index rose more than three hundred percent. Costco's price moved zero. That is not an oversight. It has survived three CEOs, two financial crises, a pandemic, and at least one serious internal proposal to raise it to a dollar seventy-five.

Here is the number that reframes everything. In fiscal year 2023, Costco reported four point five eight billion dollars in membership fee revenue against six point two nine billion in net income. Membership fees covered nearly seventy-three percent of net income. The bulk goods, the electronics, the giant muffins — those are not the business. The annual fee is the business. Everything else exists to justify renewing it.

When Craig Jelinek became CEO in 2012, co-founder Jim Sinegal passed the food court to him with one instruction. According to Jelinek, recounting the exchange in multiple public interviews, Sinegal's words were direct: "If you raise the price of the f***ing hot dog, I will kill you." Jelinek has not raised the price.

In 2009, input costs rose and Costco's hot dog supplier, Hebrew National, carried kosher certification requirements that imposed cost floors no negotiation could clear. Rather than raise the price, Costco dropped Hebrew National and launched its own Kirkland Signature beef hot dogs.

To ensure supply certainty, the company built a hot dog manufacturing facility in California capable of producing approximately one hundred million units annually. A company constructed a factory to defend a one dollar and fifty cent price point. That decision only makes sense if you are not pricing the hot dog. You are pricing the promise it represents.

Costco's US and Canada membership renewal rate has exceeded ninety percent every year since at least 2012. That figure is disclosed in every annual 10-K. Over seventy million paid members. Ninety-two point seven percent of them renew.

But here is what most case studies miss about that number.

edited_script.title: Costco's $1.50 Hot Dog — Part 1
edited_script.description: How a $1.50 price point that has not changed since 1985 became the foundation of Costco's membership retention engine.

scenes.title: Hook: $1.50 Since 1985
text_overlays: Seattle, WA · First Costco Food Court

narration.text: In 1985, a Costco warehouse in Seattle, Washington, listed a hot dog and a twenty-ounce soda for one dollar and fifty cents. That was forty-one years ago. The price today is still one dollar and fifty cents.

visuals.prompt: Overhead flat-lay illustration of a classic American hot dog in a white paper wrapper and a wax cup with soda straw, a handwritten price tag reading $1.50 resting beside it, placed on a worn formica counter surface. Warm tungsten overhead lighting, high-contrast shadows, photorealistic illustration grain. No people. No human silhouettes.

narration.text: In the same period, the US Consumer Price Index rose more than three hundred percent. Costco's price moved zero. That is not an oversight. It has survived three CEOs, two financial crises, a pandemic, and at least one serious internal proposal to raise it to a dollar seventy-five.

visuals.prompt: Cinematic data visualization illustration: a flat horizontal line labeled '$1.50' in gold running across the full frame from 1985 to 2025, while a steep upward-curving line in white above it represents CPI inflation rising 300%. Deep navy blue background, fine white grid lines, annotation callouts at 1985 and 2025. No people. No human silhouettes.

narration.text: Here is the number that reframes everything. In fiscal year 2023, Costco reported four point five eight billion dollars in membership fee revenue against six point two nine billion in net income. Membership fees covered nearly seventy-three percent of net income. The bulk goods, the electronics, the giant muffins — those are not the business. The annual fee is the business. Everything else exists to justify renewing it.

visuals.prompt: Infographic illustration: a large circular donut chart with a dominant 73% arc in deep gold labeled 'Membership Fees $4.58B' and a 27% arc in slate gray labeled 'Other Operations'. Clean sans-serif typography, white background, subtle drop shadow under the chart, dollar figure callout in center. No people. No human silhouettes.

narration.text: When Craig Jelinek became CEO in 2012, co-founder Jim Sinegal passed the food court to him with one instruction. According to Jelinek, recounting the exchange in multiple public interviews, Sinegal's words were direct: "If you raise the price of the f***ing hot dog, I will kill you." Jelinek has not raised the price.

visuals.prompt: Cinematic illustration of an empty corporate boardroom seen from a low angle: a long dark wood conference table stretching toward the far wall, a single leather chair at the head position, late afternoon sun cutting through venetian blinds casting sharp parallel shadows across the table surface. Serious, weighty atmosphere. No people. No human silhouettes.

narration.text: In 2009, input costs rose and Costco's hot dog supplier, Hebrew National, carried kosher certification requirements that imposed cost floors no negotiation could clear. Rather than raise the price, Costco dropped Hebrew National and launched its own Kirkland Signature beef hot dogs.

visuals.prompt: Studio product illustration of two hot dog packages placed side by side on a clean white surface: left package in blue and white with a kosher certification star motif, right package in dark navy and silver with a generic house-brand label. Soft overhead studio lighting, minimal shadows, product photography composition. No people. No human silhouettes.

narration.text: To ensure supply certainty, the company built a hot dog manufacturing facility in California capable of producing approximately one hundred million units annually. A company constructed a factory to defend a one dollar and fifty cent price point. That decision only makes sense if you are not pricing the hot dog. You are pricing the promise it represents.

visuals.prompt: Aerial cinematic illustration of a large industrial food manufacturing warehouse complex in the California Central Valley: flat agricultural land surrounding it, golden hour sunlight from the west casting a long building shadow eastward, loading dock bays visible along one wall, scale conveying massive operational capacity. No people. No human silhouettes.

narration.text: Costco's US and Canada membership renewal rate has exceeded ninety percent every year since at least 2012. That figure is disclosed in every annual 10-K. Over seventy million paid members. Ninety-two point seven percent of them renew.

visuals.prompt: Data visualization illustration: a horizontal trend line chart plotting Costco US/Canada membership renewal rate from 2012 to 2023, consistently above a bold 90% threshold line, colored in deep teal with annotation markers at each year. Clean infographic style, white background, bold axis labels, rate values printed at each data point. No people. No human silhouettes.

narration.text: But here is what most case studies miss about that number.

visuals.prompt: Cinematic wide-angle illustration of a Costco-style warehouse interior at golden hour: vast industrial ceiling with overhead LED lighting arrays, long perspective aisle receding toward a brightly lit food court counter in the far background, dramatic depth of field blur at the food court. Atmospheric, mysterious tone. No people. No human silhouettes.
